The thing is, I *want* the worlds of async and sync to be separate islands in Curio.  Backing that statement up for a moment....

... the earliest versions of Curio had a small collection of methods on the `Kernel` class including a `spawn()` method.  However, in thinking about that, I became concerned about muddying the waters between operations performed on the Kernel by synchronous code and operations performed by asynchronous code.  For example, is it legal to use `Kernel.spawn()` in both contexts?  Can outside threads call `Kernel.spawn()`?   I also looked at the `asyncio`Event loop and it's large zoo of functions like `run_forever()`, `run_until_complete()`, `call_soon()`, `create_task()`, and whatnot.     I consciously decided to take a different direction and make the invocation of Curio as dead simple as possible.  `run()`--that is the one and only public API function for invoking Curio from synchronous code.  

Even in the proposed code above, it gets muddy real fast.  For example, consider that `Task.val()` method.  Who can call that?  Synchronous code outside of Curio? A separate thread outside of Curio?  An asynchronous task running within Curio?   Synchronous code called by an async task currently running in Curio?  Is calling the `kernel.run()` method legal in all of these contexts?  (most likely not). 

At this point, I'm not sure I want to back out of that and start adding methods to the `Kernel` class.  If someone wants to make the kernel launch a collection of background tasks, or have it return task objects, or fool around with Futures, I feel that it can all be done in the initial coroutine given to `run()`.  Although I'm fine with `run()` having a few tweaks related to scheduling/polling for the purpose of it sitting in an outside event loop, I'd like to keep everything else involving the kernel sitting on the async side of the fence.

As far as TVP goes I think I stress of a function that generates the Socket object having even a thinnish wrapper around this for TCP/UDP/Unix sockets as like a malleable class would make working with sockets just less clunky. Right now I have an async function that generates a socket then hands it to like a “server” class if you will or an “io” class or what have you. As I see this and I think from your statements you do too in some ways but please correct me if I’m wrong - this is a library for people to build upon something that they then may share with others, it makes the downstream efforts much more easier and transparent to work with because ultimately if I produce work win this let’s say and it’s going to be used by my colleagues the less they need to stare at internals the better. That’s how I think of this anyway. Even if a TCP/UDP/Socket class just basically proxies the socket.socket() return object and matched its methods (eg sendfile for instance) they would be good enough to build on structurally speaking. I can hen further abstract that as a framework/module/library Dev.

With channels maybe think of it as a pub/sub thing kind of like zeromq. In practice it feels similar and right now I feel like it does well there but I’m looking at just cleaning that up for modularities sake perhaps?

As for the kernel: it’s kodtly related to the run method. In the docs you describe that one should use Kernel.run() in scenarios of having heavy computation or lots of different connection points (that’s how I read it anyway). If I want to customize that at all it doesn’t feel transparent to me how I should do that. Maybe decouple that to a Run class instead? Even if it just proxies Kernel.

And as a gentle aside, I usually can tell the difference if I’m suppose to touch something or not if it’s named with an underscore or two underscores. Perhaps objects we aren’t supposed to really use might be renamed to say `_Kernel` Of similar? That’s not critical obviously

I'm not adding a special `call_soon()` API to curio.   The kernel presently has the ability to wait on a `Future` so if some kind of coordination with threads is needed, it should be built on that.  

As for N curio tasks communicating with N threads and deadlock---don't do that! ;-)   The `abide()` mechanism will likely work just fine if you are doing something like communicating from a single thread to a single curio task though.  For example, communicating between Tkinter and curio.  

I'm open to new kinds of communication mechanisms if someone wants to implement something though.   There are probably some efficiencies that can be had if one makes assumptions about the endpoints of a queue.   For example, a queue that can only be read by threads and written by curio (and the other way around).

Hey, the goal of the change were primarily to make things less magical so faces don’t disappear when the user wasn’t expecting it. 

Currently, degenerate faces are currently in two buckets:

1) Faces with a cross product smaller than tol.zero are removed by remove_degenerate_faces called with no arguments. These were previously silently removed when you requested `mesh.faces` or `mesh.face_normals` which was confusing.  Thinking about it a little more, this is not the greatest metric because you can have faces with edges all above tol.merge have a cross product below tol.zero:

```
In [61]: v = trimesh.unitize([[2,3,0], [-2,3,0]]) * 1e-7

In [62]: np.linalg.norm(np.cross(*v))
Out[62]: 9.230769230769232e-15
```

2) Faces with one edge of the OBB less than a specified height, removed by remove_degenerate_faces with a height (often tol.merge). This is a nice metric because it handles the obvious cases (duplicated vertex, 3 colinear points) and has good behavior around small values:
```
n [74]: v = trimesh.unitize([[2,3,0], [-2,3,0]]) * 2e-8
In [75]: tri = np.vstack((v, [0,0,0])).reshape((-1,3,3))
In [76]: trimesh.triangles.nondegenerate(tri)
Out[76]: array([ True])

In [77]: v = trimesh.unitize([[2,3,0], [-2,3,0]]) * 1e-8
In [78]: tri = np.vstack((v, [0,0,0])).reshape((-1,3,3))
In [79]: trimesh.triangles.nondegenerate(tri)
Out[79]: array([False])
```


In [this branch](https://github.com/mikedh/trimesh/tree/degenerate), I changed the behavior to:
- Use *only* the OBB metric to declare a face degenerate
- keep validate False by default, but if True, have it use the OBB metric (ignore cross product magnitude completely)
- face normals with a cross product smaller than tol.zero will be returned as zero magnitude vectors

I think that's an improvement, though I'm open to suggestions.

Hey Vlad,

So basically the idea is as follows. When you have a simple strategy where the child nodes are securities, at algo run time, the algos have access to each security's last price (and historical price) to make their decisions ( calculating weights, momentum, etc.)

If we want the same to hold true for strategies of strategies, we need to first run the child strategy. Once it has been run, we will have its latest "price" (equity value). Once we have this information, our Algos in the master strategy will have the latest "prices" to make decisions as discussed above. Once the master has allocated capital according to its AlgoStack, then we can run the child strategies so that they can in turn deploy the capital that was allocated to them.

Basically, on new data:
1. Run child strategies (paper)
2. Run parent strategy
3. Run child strategies (real)

As you can see above, the first child run is done in a paper trading context - basically it just copies the logic of the strategy and runs it as if it was independent. We don't actually want to run the child strategy twice (extra commissions, etc.) so that's why we run a "paper trading" version of it. The only reason we have this paper strategy is to provide us with a price series for the child strategy. We will then be able to use this price series in our algos at the master strategy level.

Hope this makes sense. I'm still not sure if this is the most efficient/best way to go about this - when you get into strategies of strategies, it starts to get complex!

Let me know if you have any other questions,
Phil

Can you point me to a literature source on this? It doesn't look right. For example, here is a description of an adaptive filter; https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/mechanical-engineering/2-161-signal-processing-continuous-and-discrete-fall-2008/study-materials/rls.pdf, and I think this derives the equation presented in that paper https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/electrical-engineering-and-computer-science/6-241j-dynamic-systems-and-control-spring-2011/readings/MIT6_241JS11_chap02.pdf

Another (different) example would be filterpy.memory.FadingMemoryFilter.

Filters are designed by minimizing an error term (an approach I didn't take in the book, but nonetheless it is correct). The version in the MIT paper is derived by minimizing sum(f^[k-1] e^2), which provides an exponential decay on the error term. I'd be surprised if fixing N led to an optimal formulation or that the computation of the truncation error is now correct. Also, your push in it's current form is probably erroneous when N < alpha. 

Zarchan has a fixed memory filter that starts out like you do, having a constant term, however he also maintains an L-length (L is what you call alpha in his formulation) history of the measurements because you are essentially computing a sliding window over the past measurements. 

In any case, I'm very unlikely to accept a push of this nature. RLS can be formulated in a more general form (see the MIT paper) that looks nearly identical to the KF equations minus the predict part. And then if you aren't able to use the KF formulation, there are far more sophisticated algorithms, such as the Savitzky–Golay filter. The formulation of this and the fading memory filter are very specific to his book and how he develops his material, and I'm not particularly interested in continuing down that path. Anyone can download his Fortran and Matlab code if interested in his work.

Exactly: it just doesn't make sense to add any level of complexity in that sense, mainly because there's not a "blessed" protocol which can be chosen (SSH? HTTP? Whatever...?), and any solution you can think of will never be generic enough to satisfy all the possible use cases. All the types returned by psutil functions and methods are standard python types (int, list, dict, str, set, nameduple) which can be easily serialized (e.g. via JSON), transmitted over a certain protocol (e.g. HTTP) and recomposed on the remote endpoint in order to recreate the original Python type(s) and deal with them. How to transmit and handle that stream of data/information is entirely up to the user, not the library per se. To say one, Process.as_dict() was explicitly designed to be used in such a case (#282 and #408 comes to mind). In summary this is one of those cases where the KISS (keep it simple stupid) principle applies. =)

I think that the problem described above has to do with logging in for the first time before the first cron job has run to populate the groups.  Since the groups have not yet populated, each attempt to add the user to the group fails.  And then--apparently--it never tries to add the user again.  I have verified that a new user logging in after the groups are first populated does get added to the groups, but the first guy who logged in too early is SOL.  So the work-around for this issue as it now stands is to wait until after there's been enough time for the first cron job to run before logging in.

This error is a result of the `name` attribute of one of the servers being `None`.
This can happen when the server is unavailable, and you were told as much when you asked in the discord.py server.
Even so, this exception in `on_ready` doesn't stop the bot from loading. All it does is stop execution of the rest of your `on_ready`, which is just printing the names of the rest of the servers.
I'd recommend using string formatting or an f-string to print the name, so that it just prints "None" instead of throwing the error.

Some other things to note:
[`Client.servers`](https://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#discord.Client.servers) is already an iterable; `list(client.servers)` is redundant and unnecessary.
Instead of using `range()`, `len()`, and an index, you can just loop through the list of servers and use `server.name` directly for each server.
You have a lot of redundant code in your `on_message`. Consider using a loop or a function.
You might also want to look into using the commands extension if you plan on adding commands in the future.

In the future, questions and requests for help regarding usage of the library should stay in the discord.py server. GitHub issues are for issues in the library itself.

Please post the fragment of Python which illustrates the way in which you are connecting with pyodbc.

You are looking for "autocommit".  You can turn autocommit on when creating the connection by passing "autocommit=True" to the connect function.  Once you have a connection, you can change the status using the `autocommit` property.

```
cnxn = pyodbc.connect(..., autocommit=True)

cnxn.autocommit = False
cnxn.autocommit = True
```

This does exactly what you want, but be sure it is what you need.  The connections begin with autocommit off which ensures code is maintanable since an explicit commit is required.  If you start with one update and later add two, explicit commits will always work:

```
cnxn.execute(...)   # original code
cnxn.execute(...)   # a second update someone adds later
cnxn.commit()
```

With autocommit turned on, if the original code succeeds but the 2nd fails, the first update will be committed.  I know this is obvious, but it is common to not like autocommit=False when starting out and grow to like it over time.

Either way, good luck!

I'm not sure where you got that happybase is supposedly "no more supported".

HBase Thrift is a stable interface, and Happybase targets that interface. What "supported in happybase" means is basically that it wraps most of the features that the Thrift API offers, and that it has been tested. Newer HBase versions may add new features, which may not be accessible from happybase since they haven't been implemented in happybase (yet).

Anyway, since the HBase Thrift API is stable, current happybase versions will work just fine with newer hbase versions.

Having a "0.98" compat mode in the code that really doesn't do anything than it would do with "0.96" would just be marketing.

First value is the value the part is assigned after a reset not after a bump. A reset happens when you bump any preceding part, that is, bumping **minor** would cause patch and release_name to be reseted. 
Also remember that by default the first entry in the *values* list is treated as an optional value. Since your release_name part is optional (optional regex and alternative serialize) version 0.3.9 is understood by bumpversion as 0.3.9-foo. So bumping release_name will cause foo to increase to bar. Which is the result you get. 
If you bump patch in 0.3.9 the result should be 0.3.10-foo.

Note the following isnt a definitive reason for using logging ; I'm only suggesting this is one possible use-case which might justify the additional work/complexity.  Maybe it might also spark other ideas.

As coala uses vulture as an import instead of cli, the `logging` system processes these messages in a much more reliable manner than them appearing on stdout, especially relevant for multi-threaded/-processing environments like coala and pytest.  I must admit that coala does pipe the vulture stdout to a buffer, and it will be routed to the user via `logging` anyway, and I'm not sure off-hand when we show it to the user : probably only on errors, especially errors & failures in our tests.  coala has only recently switched over its codebase to using `logging`, so I am fairly confident it doesnt yet have additional features for linters that are also using `logging`.  But that is likely also because we dont have many linters which use `logging`, so havent had reason to improve that area.

Well, Debian has a habbit of running tests while building packages (those are nice but not revolutionary). The big change is DEP-8 tests which allow for testing of *installed* packages. I see now. Your metric only creates a warning from scikit-learn's `auc` function so you should still get output. The gplearn fitness function factory and its (incredibly basic) tests are based upon the assumption that you are doing regression as that is what is supported right now. Classification is on the release plan for 0.4.0 and will require me reworking the custom fitness design a bit. In the meantime you could work around this by using the private `_Fitness` object to create your metric manually:

    from gplearn.fitness import _Fitness
    auc_scores = _Fitness(function=auc_score, greater_is_better=True)

Have not tested this on any real problem, but it will bypass the basic tests that are done by `make_fitness` and hopefully give you a pointer in the right direction. Can't guarantee the results will make any sense at all since the estimator is a regressor and you will get potentially unbounded continuous outputs!

In short, classification is not supported yet. Hacking of the metric to make it support is on the user at this point (but hack away, that's always fun) but hang tight and it will be there sooner or later.

Note that when you are setting the `gateways` field; **assh** will iterate over all the entries of this field until the the first successful connection.
So technically, the first host is not a fallback, but the second one, the third one... are

If you want to have "fallback gateways", you need to put the magic "direct" host as the first entry of this field, so **assh** will try to connect without gateways before trying the rest of the list.

Do you find that odd? I may write a note on the README.md if this is not 100% clear as it is for me the feature I'm using the most every days :)

---

**example:**

```yaml
hosts:
  aaa:
    gateways:
    - bbb
    - ccc
    - direct
    - eee
```

`ssh aaa` will try until first success:
* `ssh bbb` -> `ssh aaa`
* `ssh ccc` -> `ssh aaa`
* `ssh aaa`
* `ssh eee` -> `ssh aaa`

This bug is probably is probably deprecated as its too old, but I'll explain what I did to customize my calendars for future reference. Start, end and slot duration on the web calendars are governed by the parameters passed to the daily_table template tag defined [here](https://github.com/llazzaro/django-scheduler/blob/develop/schedule/templatetags/scheduletags.py#L49). 

AFAIK the templates that use it are:

* [calendar_day.html](https://github.com/llazzaro/django-scheduler/blob/develop/schedule/templates/schedule/calendar_day.html#L29)
* [calendar_week.html](https://github.com/llazzaro/django-scheduler/blob/develop/schedule/templates/schedule/calendar_week.html#L36)

To customize these values you need to overwrite these templates. To do this elegantly you need to do 3 things:

1. Copy the templates in django-scheduler under your the template search path. Normally django looks under the ``templates`` subdirectory of every django app, so that would normally be under ``<your django app directory>/templates/schedule/``. The ``schedule`` subdirectory must exist as this is how all templates are referenced from within django-scheduler.
2. Customize the copied templates passing the start (hour) end (hour) and slot duration in minutes that you want, example, to start at 00, end at 24 hours and have 15 min slots you would use this:

```jinja
{% daily_table day 0 24 15 %}
```

3. Place ``scheduler`` in your django config's ``INSTALLED_APPS`` below the app that has the customized templates so they are found first and used instead of the ones in django-scheduler.

Indeed it would be simpler @EvaSDK . Having a configuration parameter for this was the second thing in my mind. Didn't thought of Docker use case otherwise it would have become the first. Workflow for that would be something like this:
* Developer has to set NGINX_VERSION parameter in django configuration with other settings like backends.
* If the NGINX_VERSION is found to be set, it is used to decide whether the URL's should be quoted or not. The decision is cached in variable as is done currently.
* If NGINX_VERSION setting is not found to be set, we assume that NGINX_VERSION is greater than 1.5.9 and thus decision is made and cached. Doing this has a benefit that people using django-sendfile currently will not be forced to make any changes on there part if everything is working fine. On other hands developers with older Nginx can use configuration setting to make Django-sendfile work with there project.

If community feels good with this change I can update the PR. Just leave me a comment regarding this.

Hello,

In hvad, an object "does not exist" if it's not translated in the requested language. There are two ways to go around this:
* Always create the translation, even if it's left empty.
* Provide a list of language fallbacks using for instance `MyModel.objects.language().fallbacks("de", "fr", "en")`. You may also call `.fallbacks()` with no arguments to have it use `settings.LANGUAGES`.

Be aware that option #2 creates a self join on the translations table in the query. It's fine performance-wise thanks to rare matching entries, but it might affect `select_related` in odd ways, please check your results when using it.

Of course you won't see the username because the body field overrides the entire POST **body** removing anything else like **form** properties. The reason you were able to use only username or any other **form** property (when `profile` is empty) is that by default DRF enables [these parsers](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/settings/#default_parser_classes)
```
(
    'rest_framework.parsers.JSONParser',
    'rest_framework.parsers.FormParser',
    'rest_framework.parsers.MultiPartParser'
)
```
This means in the first attempt `JSONParser` was failing and `FormParser` suceeded to fill `request.data` with the username. However, on the second attempt `JSONParser` worked since there is a valid json passed in the request body, therefore other from properties were ignored (as I mentioned above I doubt they were sent first place).
This should now be fixed in v2.

Rat is a script that checks license compatibility, and that we have integrated to our travis checks. It sometimes complains when more files are added as the number of files without a license must decrease.

Yes.

I missed testing such case where the pair (`bucket`,`blob`), as returned by `_parse_gcs_url` in `contrib/hooks/gcs_hook.py` would have an empty `blob`, and given that i made the requirement of a trailing '/' for such blob... this would always fail. See below:
```
>>> _parse_gcs_url("gs://")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "airflow/contrib/hooks/gcs_hook.py", line 457, in _parse_gcs_url
    raise AirflowException('Please provide a bucket name')
airflow.exceptions.AirflowException: Please provide a bucket name
>>> _parse_gcs_url("gs:///")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "airflow/contrib/hooks/gcs_hook.py", line 457, in _parse_gcs_url
    raise AirflowException('Please provide a bucket name')
airflow.exceptions.AirflowException: Please provide a bucket name
>>> _parse_gcs_url("gs://pong")
('pong', '')
>>> _parse_gcs_url("gs://pong/")
('pong', '')
>>> _parse_gcs_url("gs://pong///")
('pong', '')
>>> _parse_gcs_url("gs://pong/ping")
('pong', 'ping')
>>> _parse_gcs_url("gs://pong/ping/")
('pong', 'ping/')
>>> _parse_gcs_url("gs://pong/ /")
('pong', ' /')
```
Note how `_parse_gcs_url` removes any trailing `/` after the bucket name as this is done by the functionality given in `urlparse` (and it does keep it when there is a non-empty `blob`)

So the requirement of a trailing `/` in the operator still applies, but it should allow root directory as blob, which is the same as empty `blob`.
Allowing for no trailing `/` would complicate the logic of the operator when comparing the contents of the S3 and GCS for syncing, at least when I did this, and the normal behaviour is to sync 'directories' hence I think the semantics of a trailing '/' make it clearer and easier to understand.

Ok, great. I think you're exactly right about where the difficulties will lie.

I'm most concerned with keeping the API clean and slightly less concerned with what the implementation looks like. I think that's the best policy for library code. However, of course maintenance is a concern, and you're exactly right the 2 current implementations are similar but mostly don't share code. The reason is these were once much simpler and grew up to their present state as features were added. Looking now with a critical eye, we could at minimum factor out a few helper functions. This might help to make the 4 implementations we end up with a little less unwieldy. Maybe I'll see how far I get doing this as a first step, since it probably makes sense anyway.

> 2. Properly handle old Pythons, where asyncio is not available in backoff.py, tests, CI, etc.

I did a little exploratory coding for this, and I think the biggest problem here is going to be with new python 3 _syntax_.  If the `asyncio` library isn't available in an python 2.7, that's not a problem because we can just conditionally not import it at runtime, and conditionally not define the `retry_async` version of the retry function. But if the code failed to _compile_ because of new syntax e.g. `yield from` then it won't work and we are stuck. So the question is: is that new syntax required for the async path to work?

@blampe 

cancel and claim are designed for implementing speculative execution. They are an internal part of tchannel and hyperbahn and not really meant to be used as part of the application developer public interface.

The expected workflow is as follows: 

 - On an hyperbahn exit node, if a call request `se` header is set to `2` then:
 - hyperbahn exit node picks two random instances, A & B
 - Send call request to A with claim at finish === B
 - Wait ttl / 2, if A is not finished then send the same call request to B with claim at start === A
 - If A finishes the request then it sends claim to B, if B starts the request then it sends claim to A.
 - Claim messages are cheap to send if your service is a ringpop and is already fully connected + has open connections
 
If both A & B are slow/broken then the request will timeout.

If A is broken, but B is healthy then the request finished in ttl/2
If A is healthy then the request finishes normally.

> This seems to indicate that workers are communicating directly with each other

That is ringpop. Workers communicate directly because everything is ringpop.

Well, did you set the `SHELL` environment variable?

- This does not create reproducible results, this creates user preferences.  Pipfiles are not about user preferences, and if you want to change the spec of pipfiles you will need to take it up in @pypa/pipfile 

- The actual setting is called `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT` so you might find that you keep using the other variable because it doesn't actually do anything

- Once a virtualenv is actually created inside a project folder, pipenv detects it automatically, so there would be no reason to prepend any commands with anything

-  If your team all prefer to put virtualenv folders inside their project directories why not just set this in their `.profile` or equivalent file, I can't really think of any situation where it would make sense to continuously type this in as a prefix

What folder a person chooses to have a virtualenv created in, and what packages are required to reproduce that environment, I'm sure you can understand, are completely independent of one another.  One is a user preference, one is a reproduction necessity.  We separate these intentionally by keeping the environment reproduction isolated from user preferences so that you can pass an environment (`Pipfile` and `Pipfile.lock`) to anyone and they can install it using their own configuration and it shouldn't make any difference.

> Whether a virtualenv lives in the project itself is, by nature, as the name of the config suggests, a per-project setting, not a per-pipenv-invocation settings

Why is the folder of a virtualenv a per-project setting?  This is the entire point of pipenv.  This is abstracted away.  Where the virtualenv actually is located doesn't matter _at all_.  The fact is that pipenv manages it for you, so _wherever it is_, calling `pipenv run x` or `pipenv install y` will interact with it correctly.  Just like when you install a new set of packages from a `requirements.txt`, nothing about that tells you precisely what directory path you need to put your virtualenv in, and just like `virtualenvwrapper` allows you to specify a place to store all virtualenvs, pipenv combines theses things and manages the dependencies of a package _and_ the location of your virtualenvs.

I can understand how you came to think this would make sense, but your workflow is only one of many.  Hopefully you understand where we are coming from -- but I don't see this being added to the pipfile spec for the reasons described

Feel free to let me know if you have any questions but I'm going to close the issue for now as out of scope

One can compute the cardinality of an intersection between two (standard, uncompressed) bitsets using no more than one cycle per 64-bit word. So that's about 30-something cycles on a recent Intel CPU if you have 2048 bits. And no, using GCC's popcount intrinsic is not nearly optimal on a recent Intel processor. Your are at least 2x off, and that's if you optimized the hell out of your C code. You want to be using AVX. See the source code of CRoaring.

But you can do a hell of a lot better if the density is low using SIMD instructions and simple sorted lists. Moreover, you can compress the sorted lists for even better gains.

E.g., see http://arxiv.org/abs/1401.6399

Not that you can mix and match bitsets and lists... it is not just ine or the other (see how Roaring works).

What's best in your case?

Usually, you don't resolve this issues by thinking abstractly. You setup a benchmark and you measure.

If you don't even know the basic characteristics if the problem, like what the data looks like... then just use the simplest implementation and wait till you have actual data to optimize your use case.

@gsmcmullin @jacobmcnamee, so in every project of this kind I've seen, it's been typical to keep generated code and the compiler under version control in the same repository. I was going to list examples but it seems @mfine has already done that. I disagree pretty strongly about splitting this repository up or keeping the source code out of version control. If this is an antipattern, I'd prefer to see some more substantive arguments describing why its necessary rather than the statement that others are Wrong.

Some points:
* Having multiple repositories or separate release artifacts introduces more management overhead to keep track off than we currently have. If you think it's tedious now to code review or manage multi-language clients across directories, just wait until they're split into multiple repositories. If we generated the source code as release artifacts, most users and developers would likely download and review those separately. At this point, you'd go through a more roundabout approach to performing what we currently do. The easy thing with the current repository structure is a very flat information hierarchy: you can read through source, review it, and deploy it all from a single place. It's tedious, yes, but typically for large commits and less arguably tedious than the alternatives.
* The primary artifact here for all consumers is the generated source code. How that source code is generated is different for each client, but ultimately it's intended to be human readable and reviewed. You cannot review the generator of the code and then reason, "Yes! This AST will generate the source I want.". It is the generated source code that is meant to be reviewed and consumed by our clients, which is why it's checked into version control.
* If the generated files are out-of-sync, then we can put a procedure (git commit hook, etc.) into place that fixes that, because it definitely sounds like a problem. I totally agree.
* The time spent with repository gymnastics and yakshaving is time best spent elsewhere.

The flow is like this:

1. dx login
1. Proxy receives request and presents a valid and signed cert (signed by CA distributed and trusted by the OS)
1. ..(content inspection)...
1. Proxy creates a new request to target (`auth.dnanexus.com` in our case) and forwards payload

I guess we'd see something different than a connection timeout then. Just mentioning this in case you do some kind of certificate pinning. And the `requests` code snippet would show a different CA.

Yes this presents a MITM-attack. But in this case it is valid since it is mandated by internal regulations to be able to access all traffic, incoming as well as outgoing. I've seen this quite a bunch of times at larger clients and it almost always presents a huge challenge to get IT security to sign off on this so one is able to use service that do certificate pinning or similiar. Unfortunately the enterprise products too often **do not** modify all the required headers, let alone if the certificate pins are in the client and the public key is checked purely on client side.

@jimfulton, thanks for your reply!

`LockFileContentFormatter` is there since I didn't want to import the `socket` module nor call `socket.gethostname()` unless the host name is actually used in the template. I was mainly concerned about compatibility across platforms and exotic environments here.

If you see that always importing `socket` and calling `socket.gethostname()` is not an issue, I'll be happy to simplify that part of the implementation.

`LockFileParserFormatter` adds support for parsing and reporting both pid and hostname in case of a locking failure. We could just include the raw content of the lock file in the exception message instead, if that's ok.

I'll convert tests to use the `mock` module (which I like a lot). I was wary of bringing additional dependencies, but it's good to hear that's not a problem.

I mildly dislike the DWIMing of the first argument acting as either a filename or the test itself, but I can't convince myself that using differently named decorators or different named parameters would make it any better, so I won't complain.

WRT globals, I do think that sharing globals into inlined doctests is important (both the 1st argument tests and the decorated method docstrings), mainly because if I'm mixing doctests with regular test methods in the same test file I'd find it annoying to have to reimport modules that were imported globally only inside the inlined doctests and not in the test methods.

The external doctests I agree shouldn't share globals with the test module.

(I guess a case could be made here that the "test" global should be called (or aliased) "self" in the inlined tests)

WRT naming, `@doctestcase` seems wrong since the decorator creates a test (method), not a test-case (which is a class with several tests, a setup and a teardown). I'd also prefer a lowercased name, but anything I can think of would be weirder or less convenient than `@DocTest`.

All in all, I really like this PR for allowing doctests being able to reuse TestCase setup/teardown and being locatable by `nose`!

Thank you for the feedback.

spm_train assumes that training data is one-sentence-per-line format, meaning that the training data will never contain "\n".

Handy workaround to recognize "\n" is as follows:
1. Prepare a custom user defined symbols for "\n", for instance "<n>"
2. Runs spm_train with --user_defined_symbols="<n>" to recognize <n> as one symbol in any context.
3. Escapes "\n" in the input sentence into "<n>".

% ./spm_train --input=../data/botchan.txt --model_prefix=m --user_defined_symbols='<n>' --vocab_size=1000
% echo "hello<n>world" | ./spm_encode --model=m.model 
▁he ll o <n> w or l d

Note that the symbol "<n>" must be unique enough. Otherwise, user may change the behavior by putting "<n>" in their request :)

I was investigating on this issue and I found out that the default property should be `"true"` (with quotes) in your fist example and then it would work. But I tried to run locally to reproduce the fix, but I did not worked. So I dig into more on that issue...

I found out that the Swagger model converts the `default` property to a [native boolean value](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-core/blob/master/modules/swagger-models/src/main/java/io/swagger/models/parameters/AbstractSerializableParameter.java#L198). And since the Connexion Codegen [simply serializes the swagger definition to `yml`](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-codegen/blob/master/modules/swagger-codegen/src/main/java/io/swagger/codegen/languages/FlaskConnexionCodegen.java#L298) to be used when running the app by Connexion it breaks.

If you change the generated `yml` default value to `"true"` (with quotes) it works.

I guess that is a major issue in [all generators that output swagger definitions like that](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-codegen/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=Yaml.mapper%28%29.writeValueAsString).

I'm not able to reproduce on my side. Could you check which version of the `carto` Python package you have?

My guess is that you have version 1.0.1, while cartoframes needs at least 1.1.0: https://github.com/CartoDB/cartoframes/blob/eabd22dd2ad4de4444955af5273d776fd8b9b79f/setup.py#L18

That package was updated in v1.1.0 to allow kwargs like `skipfields` that allow avoiding the duplicate `the_geom_webmercator` column.

Reflecting on this approach, I'm thinking that we might have been too expansive.

The purpose of this feature is for giving users access to datasets for use in examples. They don't need the full functionality of a CartoContext. I can actually see some negatives:

1. Preview of datasets is not very important as they will be doing that through the code examples, so giving access to `CartoContext.map` is not needed.
2. If a user has a `CartoContext`, they may think that they would have access to `CartoContext.data{,_discovery,_boundaries}`.
3. Users may be more likely to ping the database with `CartoContext.query`, which isn't necessarily a big deal but it's not necessarily productive. For people who want to poke around it's still there ;)

Therefore, `CartoContext.read` has all needed functionality for example loading (e.g., it has a `limit=N` kwarg if only a subset is needed).

So how about something more like the following (inspired by [scikit-learn](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/datasets/index.html#toy-datasets):

```python
from cartoframes.examples import load_taxi
# or
from cartoframes import examples

taxi_df = load_taxi()
# or
taxi_df = examples.load_taxi()
```

`load_taxi` uses the example user format you proposed before, but it's hidden for the user:

```python
example_context = CartoContext(
    base_url='...',
    api_key='default_public')

def load_taxi(limit=None):
    """load taxi dataset..."""
    return example_context.read('taxi_50k', limit=limit)
```
This takes care of the curation issue (we're only giving access functions for a handful of datasets) and doesn't provide direct access to all the methods of the `example_context` object.

I think this is to be expected, due to overhead of additional imports and better serialization.

It might be possible to squeeze the time down a bit, with some effort.

Note that you can save some time by using `multiprocess` (the direct fork of `multiprocessing`) instead of `pathos`…

```
In [1]: def foo(x): return 2*x

In [2]: import multiprocessing as mp

In [3]: import multiprocess as ms

In [4]: import pathos.multiprocessing as pa

In [5]: mpp = mp.Pool(8)

In [6]: msp = ms.Pool(8)

In [7]: pap = pa.ProcessingPool(8)

In [8]: %timeit mpp.map(foo, range(100))
1000 loops, best of 3: 579 us per loop

In [9]: %timeit msp.map(foo, range(100))
100 loops, best of 3: 9.32 ms per loop

In [10]: %timeit pap.map(foo, range(100))
100 loops, best of 3: 15.4 ms per loop

In [11]: import dill

In [12]: dill.settings['recurse'] = True

In [13]: %timeit msp.map(foo, range(100))
100 loops, best of 3: 14.2 ms per loop

In [14]: mpp.close()

In [15]: msp.close()

In [16]: pap.close()

In [17]: pap.clear() 
```

And, looking purely at the underlying serialization…

```
In [1]: import pickle

In [2]: def foo(x): return 2*x

In [3]: %timeit pickle.dum
pickle.dump   pickle.dumps  

In [3]: %timeit pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(foo))
10000 loops, best of 3: 23.3 us per loop

In [4]: import dill as pickle

In [5]: %timeit pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(foo))
1000 loops, best of 3: 263 us per loop

In [6]: pickle.settings['recurse'] = True

In [7]: %timeit pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(foo))
1000 loops, best of 3: 401 us per loop 
```

You can see `dill` is slower than `pickle`.  I think it's the cost of better serialization.  I'm also sure that everything in `dill`, `multiprocess`, and `pathos` is not speed optimized.  Feel free to dig and improve if you like.

I think the core issue is what we want to implement is a reverse proxy, but not a transparent proxy.

A transparent proxy is easy to implement because it only need forward the data passed by client and forward the reply from remote to client, like a pipe, no upper level protocol understand needed. 

@wingerted and me are implemented prototype TCP and UDP transparent proxy on each one's develop branch. I tried to forward all unparseable data to 8.8.8.8:53 and I can have a success DNS query on shadowsocks server port.

The reverse proxy should know  the upper level protocol, unpack and replace the server/client details in the request, then repack it and send to remote server, after receive the response, it should do same process then send to client. It need to know all details about all protocol and have ability to modify the data pack.

If a upper level protocol doesn't carry any data about remote server, like DNS, reverse proxy can works like transparent proxy. But if it contains server related information, like HTTP (should provide host in HTTP protocol), transparent cannot works.

Implement a all protocol compatible reverse proxy seems difficult or impossible, also not the aim of shadowsocks. Do you have any new ideas about reverse proxy? Thanks.

So I was initially thinking we'd do this by leveraging `--binaries-baseurls`, with a more sophisticated interpolation scheme. But I like your way better - basically a BinaryTool can optionally say "there is a canonical place to fetch me from", and fall back to "there is no canonical place, so Pants are hosting me".

However this means that some binary tools use `--binaries-baseurls` and others don't. It's a bit weird to pass urls around as arguments, in a class that also has its own way of finding urls, that may or may not actually be used. 

The underlying design flaw, I think, is that `BinaryUtil` does two orthogonal things: it computes URLs for requested binaries, and it handles fetching.  It might be best to break it up into two separate pieces, along those lines. Then the distinction between "how we get a URL" (which used to only have one answer but now has two)  and "how we download" gets clarified.

Ok, so I've been digging in to this and pyyaml's behaviour is actually perfectly fine. It only omits quotes when the number could not parse as a valid octal number - for example, if it contains a digit higher than '7'.

```
>>> print(yaml.dump({"test with an 8": "012345678"}))
{test with an 8: 012345678}

>>> print(yaml.dump({"test with no 8": "012345677"}))
{test with no 8: '012345677'}
```

Which pyyaml then correctly loads back in as strings in both cases:

```
>>> print(yaml.load("{test with an 8: 012345678}"))
{'test with an 8': '012345678'}

>>> print(yaml.load("{test with no 8: '012345677'}"))
{'test with no 8': '012345677'}
```

So given that the output from pyyaml is correct, valid yaml with no ambiguity (a sequence of digits that begins with '0' but contains invalid octal digits, e.g. '8' or '9' is correctly interpreted as a string)...

@rtibbles Even with the new implementation, I run into the same issue: the outgoing dynamically rendered content isn't taken into account by the newly rendered content during reflow (the transitions don't apply).

I'm looking into it right now. It seems like it has to do with the way changes are detected. Vue says it uses the FLIP method to detect changes, and has an example here:

https://vuejs.org/v2/guide/transitions.html#List-Move-Transitions

You can see that they use `position: absolute` to let the inline elements on either side of the removed number start flowing into position at the same time.  I put together a codepen to copy and paste the code they used there:

I think I would probably rather use webpack for more of this type asset management - the django-webpack-loader handles the integration between the webpack bundled packages and Django (with which I am using an .ini file to configure packages that we can read either with Django or Node).

My plan was for all the front end bundle configuration to happen here, so we could define whether the frontend plugin should be registered as loading at page load or as asynchronously loading.

If frontend code is only used in its own templates, it is easy - but we would probably need a way to specify that frontend code should be loaded into particular templates (either in the core app, or in other plugins). This becomes apparent if we consider say, the 'learn' app, where we will have different content viewing plugins (perseus exercises, videos, vector videos, mp3 player, pdf, etc.) that need to be registered as available for asynchronous loading on the learn page.

My intuition is that we could specify in the ini file **app** and **template** parameters (along with sync or async) that would then load those frontend assets for that frontend plugin into the that template, e.g.:

`
[vector-video]
entry_file = js/vector-video-player.js
app = learn
template = learn.html
load = async
app = coachreports
template = preview.html
load = async
`

Or something similar (also happy to shift from ini file use, or different syntax here) this would register the frontend plugin to be loaded asynchronously in the learn.html template of the learn app, and the preview template of the coachreports app (this would probably not happen in practice, as any previewing on the coachreports should be done leveraging the learn app, but I use as an example).

If we install `pyopenssl` 16.2.0, the error `AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'SSL_ST_INIT'` will go away. So is it possible to bundle the 16.2.0 `pyopenssl` module without dependencies into Kolibri? Or maybe this won't work well, since we don't want to keep extending the size of kolibri installers?

After looking into this, I do see that wget is generally slightly faster--sometimes it's about on par. The difference I'm seeing on my end is pretty easily explained by the fact that the ReportDownloader is constructing and encoding the SOAP body sent in the request. In contrast, there's no processing being done with the equivalent wget request because all of the fields were manually provided.

That said, your report sounds a bit more extreme. The most plausible idea that comes to mind is that you may be using some sort of proxy with the Python Client Library that may be impacting the download speed.

Given that I can't identify the specific issue on my end and there have been no further reports, I will close this issue for now.

@DZabavchik rgd what are "realistic" values for ping period.

This can't be answered in general, because it depends on what you want to achieve. In my experience, there are three main reasons for doing ping/pong:

1. fast detection of connection loss
2. keeping connections alive
3. keeping connections snappy

(1) is about full connection loss (mainly mobile these days)
(2) is about intermediaries (NATs, firewalls, proxies) timing out connections
(3) is about keeping the link and endpoint states in a mode where messages are transmitted with low latency

The last one in particular:

* http://lists.w3.org/Archives/Public/ietf-http-wg/2012JanMar/1083.html
* https://eggert.org/students/chemmagate-thesis.pdf
* http://www.stevesouders.com/blog/2011/09/21/making-a-mobile-connection/

Note, that for (3) on HSPA, it's not enough to have a quite short ping period. You also need sufficient traffic.

In my experiments, I had to use a ping period of 200 ms (!) with a payload of at least 50 octets (effective bandwith 4kb/s) to keep my mobile (on HSPA) in the most responsive state (HSPA DCH).

However, this will not only depend on the wireless standard (HSPA vs LTE), but also depend on the specific carrier network.

The mobile network of a carrier do have state machines with carrier specific configuration: http://stevesouders.com/images/rrc-load-times.png

- generate an rsa key `ssh-keygen -t -rsa -C "soft-matter-developers"`, give it a name (here `soft-matter-docs-deploy`)
- make a github Deploy Key using the public key on the correct repository
- copy over the private key to the repository that will do the doc build
- add the private key filename `soft-matter-docs-deploy` to `.gitignore`
- install travis commandline utility `gem install travis`
- encrypt the private key `travis encrypt-file soft-matter-docs-deploy --repo soft-matter/pims`
- add the line to Travis build as suggested by the travis encrypt-file tool
- also add the following lines after that:

```
openssl ........(given by travis encrypt-file tool)
eval `ssh-agent -s`
chmod 600 soft-matter-docs-deploy
ssh-add soft-matter-docs-deploy

In short, supposing you had the following meta:

```
package:
  name: foobar
  version: 1

build:
  script:
    - mkdir $PREFIX/bin
    - echo "#!$PREFIX/bin/path/to/some/script" > $PREFIX/bin/my_script
```

The aim is to be able to deploy the built package to one location, but pretend that it is actually being deployed elsewhere. You might do this with:

```
conda build .

mkdir -p /tmp/tmp_pkgs/foobar
tar -xjf /path/to/foobar-1-0.tar.bz2 -C /tmp/tmp_pkgs/foobar

python ./install.py --link \
             -p /tmp/actual_prefix \
             --target-prefix=/usr/conda \
             --pkgs-dir=/tmp/tmp_pkgs/ \
             foobar
```

At this point, you would want the ``bin/my_script`` to point to ``/usr/conda`` and *not* ``/tmp/actual_prefix``.

Guessing this is related to not having the powershell wrapper worked out yet (https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/6471), and now having a `conda.bat` on path.

The way we got around a problem in another case was to use `conda.exe` instead of `conda`, so change your

    conda create -n testenv ...

line to 

    conda.exe create -n testenv ...

The `conda.exe` solution is hopefully temporary.  But should at least work to smooth over the transitioning back and forth between 4.3 and 4.4.

In my case the problem was solved by putting 'deactivate' at the beginning of installation script, since all python instructions at Travis happen under Python virtualenv. I used miniconda in my installation script to create own virtualenv. Although PATH, PYTHONPATH variables are set perfectly, IPython picks '$VIRTUAL_ENV' variable and inserts at 'site-packages' directory from the VIRTUAL_ENV to sys.path. So in my case old numpy from Travis Virtualenv was conflicting with matplotlib I've installed in my conda environment. After 'deactivate'-ing the error has gone.

Samples follows a mapping protocol for iteration (defaults to keys), while file iteration follows a sequence protocol (defaults to records).  There is an argument to changing iteration over samples to a sequence protocol, but it would require a two-stage indexing operation (first to find the sample index, the second to extract the index).

A teaser from `requests` documentation: http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#timeouts

They suggest that **Most requests to external servers should have a timeout attached**.

My opinion is:
1. We should keep existing client *timeout strategy* at least in aiohttp 3.X
2. *connection-timeout* + *read-timeout* is just an other feasible strategy. Let's assume you are requesting an other server for an answer. Doesn't matter how often it sends a portion of data you have a strong SLA for rejecting the response if it takes too long. This is the *real case* implemented by aiohttp right now.
3. We should support flexible timeouts. I believe after aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/2768 landing we will satisfy all needs 
4. We should document timeouts **explicitly**: if somebody if not satisfied by *default provided* ones this guy should get comprehensive information about alternative strategies.

P.S.
I don't want to get a breakage like aiobotocore and personally @thehesiod had.
I pretty sure that migration should be smooth. 
I'm not convinced that defaults are so bad yet -- but I a strong request from **alternative timeout settings**, pretty well documented and very visible to newcomers.

Woohoo, I am done with this I think! This adds a bunch of tests and documentation and in general makes things a lot cleaner. Importantly:

* all the nbgrader apps now either inherit from a `BaseNbGraderApp` (which sets various defaults, like the ipython profile to be 'nbgrader'), or `BaseNbConvertApp` (which inherits from both `NbConvertApp` in IPython and `BaseNbGraderApp`)
* there is now an `AssignmentExporter` that is used by the apps that inherit from `BaseNbConvertApp`, which makes sure to include assignment-relevant things in the resources dict, including the assignment id, student id, and information about the database.
* the `--recursive` option has been removed, because I think it was only really important for `nbgrader autograde`, but in practice it's easier to just use `nbgrader autograde` on one submission at a time, and write scaffolding code around it to loop over all the students/submissions.

This significantly reduces the amount of code duplication, make nbgrader easier to understand, and also more robust, I think. I will merge this once https://github.com/ipython/ipython/pull/7912 is merged (the tests here will fail until that one is merged).

This is the result of the split between IPython and Jupyter. The notebook server is Jupyter, but the kernel you are using happens to be IPython (as opposed to IJulia, etc.). You should add the extension to your extensions to be loaded by IPython, which is most easily done via IPython configuration: In `~/.ipython/profile_default/ipython_kernel_config.py` add

```python
c.IPKernelApp.extensions = ['mymodule.extension']
```

@feanil  @jibsheet  @rocioar Adding the transaction around the aggregation introduced a number of deadlock errors.  Rather than try to resolve them, I instead opted to create StaleCompletion objects, and leave the aggregation to a single aggregator run (via the run_aggregator_service command) after the whole migration is complete.  This way there is no chance of introducing deadlocks, as the StaleCompletion objects are created unconditionally.  Then when aggregation is run, one task will be created per enrollment, so there's no chance of overlap.

@simonklb Ah, yes, this is a source of confusion.

> So, currently, a state being set in an interface with a unit scope will include multiple "conversations". Basically indicating which unit-relation is in a certain state and which aren't.
> 
> Unit A can be related to unit B and unit C.
> Conversation A-B can be in state connected and available
> while conversation A-C can be in state connected only.

Whilst, conceptually, this is true, it is not handled by the states _per se_, unless the interface code explicitly handles multiple conversations.

A naive interface may become `{relation-name}.available` when only one of the connected conversations has all the available data.  This may, or may not be, a valid state.  It's up to the interface author to ensure that the states set by the interface represent a valid description of the state of the interface **or** the interface provides functions to discover the more fine granularity of the different conversations (if that's important).

e.g. an interface could set `{relation-name}.connected` when the first conversations connects, but not set `{relation-name}.available` until n-from-m conversations are valid (due to the data on those conversations), if a quorum were necessary (say).  It might then provide a set of methods to expose which were available (if that's important), or a single method with all of the relevant data that the charm layer might need to configure the managed service.

What charms.reactive _doesn't_ do is manage states by conversation.  There is no relationship between a particular conversation and a state.  The states are at the _interface_ level rather than the _interface/conversation_ level.

btw, I am spending a lot of time these days working with records databases for my group activities....grants, people, appointments etc..   

Something that I am enjoying using is making simple filter functions and then chaining them.  For example, I want all my grant applications that are (current AND funded) so I do this by:
`grdb = load_up("grdb")`
`use1 = is_current(grdb)`
`use = is_key_has_value("application status","approved")`

This sequence would give AND.  For OR I would give grdb as the argument in each case, and then I would `chain` the results.

For me this is proving to be a powerful and intuitive way to do the munging.  Not sure if it scales performance-wise and so on,  so building complicated mongo queries and then applying them may in one go may end up being better, but for now, this is working for me.

On your first question of implementing a new DB backend: yes, it's quite straightforward. The new DB backend just needs to implement the following interface: https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/master/server/store/adapter/adapter.go
Take a look here for a sample implementation: https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/master/server/db/rethinkdb/adapter.go

I don't think it matters if you use a single backend, like MySQL or a mixed one (Cassandra + Postgres). You just write an appropriate adapter.

Regarding Redis, I would not advise it because it's really not a persistent store. It's rather an in-memory DB with snapshotting for persistence. But it's really up to you.

Very interesting discussion. I think a dice/jaccard loss would make a great addition to lasagne. My implementation of the dice coefficients helped me beat the current state of the art in BraTS 2015 (2016 has no online evaluation platform available). 
You can see my results here: https://www.smir.ch/BRATS/Start2015 in section testing evaluation, my submission is placed 1st, Kayalibay et al (which is the second arxiv paper you mentioned) is placed 4th, although I would argue they should actually be placed second since the other two submissions do not cover all test cases and is it easy to cheat by not submitting everything. The difference between their and my results are very small, I guess both loss formulations yield approximately equivalent results.
You are right that we need to find a consensus about these formulations. Unfortunately, the literature is already pretty much covered by what you already posted (there is another MICCAI paper on the importance of skip connections that makes use of a dice loss) and there is no consensus here. I always boils down to the definition of 'union', 'intersection' and `|A|` in case of either A or B (or maybe even both) being non-binary. I think we should discuss these three separately.
Here is a repetition of the definition Jan wrote earlier, just sorted by measure:

'intersection'
1) `intersection(A,B) = sum(min(A,B))`
2) `intersection(A,B) = sqrt(sum((A*B)**2))`
3) `intersection(A,B) = sum(A*B)`
If either A or B is a binary vector (which is the case here) then 3) is equivalent to 1). I am not sure which formulation between 1)/3) and 2) is better. They basically differ in the way the pixel-wise similarities between A and B are accumulated.

'union'
1) `union(A,B) = sum(max(A,B))`
2) `union(A,B) = (sum(A**2) + sum(B**2))`
here I would also like to add my own implementation:
3) 'union(A,B) = sum(A) + sum(B) - intersect(A,B)' where `intersect(A,B)` follows 3) above
Again, if either a or B is binary, 1) and 3) should be equivalent.
Here I disagree with 2) since it follows more the definition of dice than jaccard. I would reformulate 2) as `union(A,B) = sqrt(sum(A**2)) + sqrt(sum(B**2)) - intersect(A,B)` where intersect is case 2) from above. I added sqrt to be consistent with the intersect

`|A|`
1) `|A| = sum(A**2)`
2) `|A| = sum(A)`
or you could even use
3) `|A| = sqrt(sum(A**2))`


If we consider 1) and 3) for intersect and union to be equivalent then there are only two alternatives left
a) `jacc(A,B) = intersect(A,B)/union(A,B) = sqrt(sum((A*B)**2)) / (sqrt(sum(A**2)) + sqrt(sum(B**2)) - sqrt(sum((A*B)**2)))'  (here we could also argue whether the sqrt is necessary or not)
b) `jacc(A,B) = intersect(A,B)/union(A,B) = sum(A*B) / (sum(A) + sum(B) - sum(A*B))'

Using the UNet road classification example I could run the experiment using both terms and compare performance, however that would take a while since I am going on vacation on friday and need all my GPU power before that. If you can wait that long (~3 weeks) then I will happily do it (I have in interest in findung the optimal solution myself). If someone else does it until then I will be happy as well ;-)

@dataintensiveapplication follow is my code, enjoy it.

```
#!/usr/bin/env python
#coding:utf-8
# vi:tabstop=4:shiftwidth=4:expandtab:sts=4

from collections import OrderedDict

import numpy as np

import theano
import theano.tensor as T
from theano.sandbox.rng_mrg import MRG_RandomStreams as RandomStreams
from lasagne import utils
import lasagne

def get_or_compute_grads(loss_or_grads, params):
    """Helper function returning a list of gradients

    Parameters
    ----------
    loss_or_grads : symbolic expression or list of expressions
        A scalar loss expression, or a list of gradient expressions
    params : list of shared variables
        The variables to return the gradients for

    Returns
    -------
    list of expressions
        If `loss_or_grads` is a list, it is assumed to be a list of
        gradients and returned as is, unless it does not match the length
        of `params`, in which case a `ValueError` is raised.
        Otherwise, `loss_or_grads` is assumed to be a cost expression and
        the function returns `theano.grad(loss_or_grads, params)`.

    Raises
    ------
    ValueError
        If `loss_or_grads` is a list of a different length than `params`, or if
        any element of `params` is not a shared variable (while we could still
        compute its gradient, we can never update it and want to fail early).
    """
    if any(not isinstance(p, theano.compile.SharedVariable) for p in params):
        raise ValueError("params must contain shared variables only. If it "
                         "contains arbitrary parameter expressions, then "
                         "lasagne.utils.collect_shared_vars() may help you.")
    if isinstance(loss_or_grads, list):
        if not len(loss_or_grads) == len(params):
            raise ValueError("Got %d gradient expressions for %d parameters" %
                             (len(loss_or_grads), len(params)))
        return loss_or_grads
    else:
        return theano.grad(loss_or_grads, params,disconnected_inputs='warn')


def sgd(loss_or_grads, params, learning_rate, grads_clip=False):
    """Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) updates

    Generates update expressions of the form:

    * ``param := param - learning_rate * gradient``

    Parameters
    ----------
    loss_or_grads : symbolic expression or list of expressions
        A scalar loss expression, or a list of gradient expressions
    params : list of shared variables
        The variables to generate update expressions for
    learning_rate : float or symbolic scalar
        The learning rate controlling the size of update steps

    Returns
    -------
    OrderedDict
        A dictionary mapping each parameter to its update expression
    """
    grads = get_or_compute_grads(loss_or_grads, params)
    updates = OrderedDict()

    for param, grad in zip(params, grads):
        if type(grads_clip)==float and grads_clip>0:
            grad = T.clip(grad,utils.floatX(-grads_clip),utils.floatX(grads_clip))
        elif grads_clip:
            grad = T.clip(grad,utils.floatX(-1.0),utils.floatX(1.0))
        updates[param] = param - learning_rate * grad

    return updates

def apply_momentum(updates, params=None, momentum=0.9, average=1):

    N=average

    if params is None:
        params = updates.keys()
    updates = OrderedDict(updates)

    #count = theano.shared(np.zeros((1,), dtype='int8'))
    #updates[count]=(count+1) % N
    for param in params:
        value = param.get_value(borrow=True)
        sumval = theano.shared(np.zeros(value.shape, dtype=value.dtype),
                                 broadcastable=param.broadcastable)
        velocity = theano.shared(np.zeros(value.shape, dtype=value.dtype),
                                 broadcastable=param.broadcastable)
        count = theano.shared(np.zeros(value.shape, dtype='int8'),
                                 broadcastable=param.broadcastable)
        updates[count]=(count+1) % N

        avg=(sumval+updates[param])/N

        x = momentum * velocity + avg

        updates[sumval] = T.switch(T.eq(count,N-1),T.zeros_like(sumval),sumval+updates[param])
        updates[velocity] = T.switch(T.eq(count,N-1),x - param,velocity)
        updates[param] = T.switch(T.eq(count,N-1),x,param)

    return updates

def momentum(loss_or_grads, params, learning_rate, momentum=0.9, average=1, grads_clip=False):

    updates = sgd(loss_or_grads, params, learning_rate)
    return apply_momentum(updates, momentum=momentum, average=average)

def adamax(loss_or_grads, params, learning_rate=0.002, beta1=0.9,
           beta2=0.999, epsilon=1e-8, average=1, grads_clip=False, noise=False):

    N=average

    all_grads = get_or_compute_grads(loss_or_grads, params)
    t_prev = theano.shared(utils.floatX(0.))
    updates = OrderedDict()

    # Using theano constant to prevent upcasting of float32
    one = T.constant(1)

    t = t_prev + 1
    a_t = learning_rate/(one-beta1**t)

    srng = RandomStreams(lasagne.random.get_rng().randint(1, 2147462579))

    for param, g_t in zip(params, all_grads):
        value = param.get_value(borrow=True)
        if type(noise)==float and noise>0:
            g_t = g_t+srng.normal(value.shape,avg=0.0,std=noise)
        elif noise:
            g_t = g_t+srng.normal(value.shape,avg=0.0,std=1e-8)

        if type(grads_clip)==float and grads_clip>0:
            g_t = T.clip(g_t,utils.floatX(-grads_clip),utils.floatX(grads_clip))
        elif grads_clip:
            g_t = T.clip(g_t,utils.floatX(-1.0),utils.floatX(1.0))
        m_prev = theano.shared(np.zeros(value.shape, dtype=value.dtype),
                               broadcastable=param.broadcastable)
        u_prev = theano.shared(np.zeros(value.shape, dtype=value.dtype),
                               broadcastable=param.broadcastable)
        sumval = theano.shared(np.zeros(value.shape, dtype=value.dtype),
                                 broadcastable=param.broadcastable)
        count = theano.shared(np.zeros(value.shape, dtype='int8'),
                                 broadcastable=param.broadcastable)
        updates[count]=(count+1) % N

        avg=(sumval+g_t)/N

        m_t = beta1*m_prev + (one-beta1)*avg
        u_t = T.maximum(beta2*u_prev, abs(avg))
        step = a_t*m_t/(u_t + epsilon)

        updates[sumval] = T.switch(T.eq(count,N-1),T.zeros_like(sumval),sumval+g_t)
        updates[m_prev] = T.switch(T.eq(count,N-1),m_t,m_prev)
        updates[u_prev] = T.switch(T.eq(count,N-1),u_t,u_prev)
        updates[param] = T.switch(T.eq(count,N-1),param - step,param)

    count = theano.shared(0)
    updates[count]=(count+1) % N
    updates[t_prev] = T.switch(T.eq(count,N-1),t,t_prev)
    return updates

```

There are a few ways to do it.

1. Using `ast.replace` allows you to replace subtrees in the AST with other ASTs. There's also a version of that function (`ast.replace_dict`) that takes a dictionary as a replacement mapping.
2. By creating a Claripy Backend (http://angr.io/api-doc/claripy.html#claripy.backend.Backend). This will allow you to easily process the tree with your backend visitor and return a new AST as the backend object.
3. By manually recreating the AST. You can use the `claripy.ast.BV` constructor and pass in the operation and arguments.

If you know all the constants, then the first option is probably easiest, by passing in a dict of constants->replacements. If the constants are something you have to process, I would go with the second option. The third option is kind of a hack.

There has been more recent discussion about implementing a MODWT (yay!), with a DFT approach being suggested in #200.

My first instinct would be that this could be slower than a naive convolution, as wavelet filters tend to be small and the corresponding signal quite large. Taking the DFT of the filter and signal, interpolating the filter DFT out to the same length as the signal, doing the per-element multiply, and then the inverse DFT might well be slower than just doing `len(filter) * len(signal)` operations in the naive version. This is pure speculation, so benchmarks are very much needed.
Also, I think it should be fine to declare all of the new functions imported from `cwt.h` in `c_wt.pxd` as `nogil` to be in line with the changes proposed for the discrete transforms in #188.

since you only enable stream with type specified but not the format, so it will return the default value for you which is "RS2_FORMAT_RAW16" that's the reason why you encounter the problem. `​ rs2::config cfg;`
` //cfg.enable_stream(RS2_STREAM_COLOR); //this will cause seg fault in Mat(...)`

` rs2::pipeline_profile pp = pipe.start(cfg);`
` rs2::stream_profile sp = pp.get_stream(RS2_STREAM_COLOR);`
``

I don't recommending scaling down reward function, since it implies that you are changing the definition of RL problem, but if it works well for your problem thats great.

@Breakend 
We did experiments where we used Adam in Value Function instead of KFAC and it turned out not to work as well compared to using KFAC. See figure 5 a), b) in paper.
 
Regarding solving NaN's in Value Function, I suggest you try:

1. If NaN in value function happens at the very beginning, it can happen because of the first 50 cold sgd iterations, before it becomes kronecker-factored trust region. I suggestion you set max_grad_norm variable to 1.0 (or play with this hyperparam) in here https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/master/baselines/acktr/value_functions.py#L25 that will clip the gradients for sgd during first 50 iterations.

2. Reduce the learning rate and cold learning rate in value function https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/master/baselines/acktr/value_functions.py#L22
or try annealing down the learning rate.

3. Change the initialization in the last layer in the value function https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/master/baselines/acktr/value_functions.py#L16 from U.normc_initializer(1.0) to None.

Few additional current CoAP engine shortcomings that I came across:
1) No default request handler - it allows more efficient handling (and smaller code) when CoAP resources are in tree-like structure with many nodes, which require common processing.

Basically echoing the above- The regexes used to validate barcode data in [escpos-php](https://github.com/mike42/escpos-php) don't mind whether the checksums are valid, they were just added to avoid the creation of invalid ESC/POS.

I think that a well-behaved driver should avoid sending things that the device is not likely to understand. This is also useful for debugging (eg. in mike42/escpos-php#113, an exception was thrown when the CODE128 encoding was not included).

Some more info about barcodes that I've got from the PHP driver-
- The open source Android app [zxing/zxing](https://github.com/zxing/zxing) is excellent for verifying printed barcodes.
- This example ([PHP](https://github.com/mike42/escpos-php/blob/7029b7bdc5b79dbfc81c2364720187b671e53be7/example/barcode.php), [binary ESC/POS blob](https://github.com/mike42/escpos-php/raw/56afef6601a702b152c7fef8ee992a9cc383bf9d/test/integration/resources/output/barcode.bin)) prints a receipt containing a variety of barcode types.
- I'm planning to write feature requests up for escpos-php shortly to add-
 - a wrapping function for each of the nine types of barcode, accepting the data as one paramater, and any type-specific options as the other parameters, so that the driver does not expose how the barcode data is represented in ESC/POS.
 - image-based fallback for printers that can't handle barcodes themselves.

Hi Nick,

Your plan sounds like a good one, although you should not need to remove your git PmagPy directory.  You don't even need to uninstall Enthought Python, you just need to install Anaconda Python and give it priority (by commenting out Enthought Python from your .bashrc or .bash_profile).

With Anaconda, you will be able to switch easily between Python 2 and Python 3 if needed.  Likewise, with the developer install, you can switch easily between the Python 2 PmagPy branch and master.  So you will be able to access both pretty simply.  

Having PmagPy in your PATH via the developer install means that your system will access whichever version of a program your git branch is pointing to.  So, to switch between using PmagPy with Python 2 and Python 3, you will need to do just two things.  

Let's say you have Anaconda installed with python 3 as your default Python, and that you have the developer install already.  If you need to switch back to using Python 2.7 and the Python 2 compatible PmagPy, you can follow these steps:

    # create a Python 2 conda environment (one time only)
    conda create python2_env python=python2.7
    # switch to using that python 2 environment
    source activate python2_env    
    # install any needed packages into this environment (one time only)
    # for example:
    conda install numpy
    pip install --upgrade --pre -f https://wxpython.org/Phoenix/snapshot-builds/ wxPython_Phoenix
    # switch to the Python2 branch (must be in your PmagPy git directory)
    git checkout Python2

To go back to your defaults of Python 3 and PmagPy for Python 3:

    source deactivate
    git checkout master

As far as the standalones are concerned, we just created up-to-date versions for all platforms from the Python 2 branch.  Once master is stable again, you are correct, we will start making new releases off of master.  

And as far as the release branch is concerned, that is just a temporary branch that I create when it is time to do a new release.  What this does is freeze development while I (or whomever else) does some testing on that branch to make sure everything is ok, while still allowing other developers to continue making changes to master.  After making a new release, the release branch gets deleted.

Let me know if you have any additional questions!  Hopefully this should be pretty straightforward.

I use cmd2 since 1 year (when I started to write in Python). My own old interpreter in Pascal understood abbreviated commands, as well. My experience: It accelerates work without disadvantage. Moreover, in cmd2 abbreviation has been a switchable feature. Users who did not like it, switched it off. When I installed my program on a new computer, it did not work because of this change without notice. I expected a warning, when the user has set "self.abbrev = True". To avoid further unpleasant surprise, I now provide my users with a "frozen" cmd2. I suggest different politics: Do not remove features.

Some useful features which I have realized in my program are (additional) argument separation by comma, passing of arguments to called scripts and script nesting (including nested passing of script arguments). Interested in this Python code of a novice? Using the program I have just processed 360 GB of X-ray scattering data without any problem introduced by abbreviated commands.

monkey-patching with backports.match_hostname did not work for me... but fortunately there is a simpler approach. If you only need to communicate with the kubernetes master you can define your own match_hostname function.

```python
def _MatchHostname(_, hostname):
  if hostname != os.environ['KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST']:
    raise ssl.CertificateError('Hostname [%s] not equal to [%s].',
        hostname, os.environ['KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST'])
```

Now monkey patch with this and it appears to work fine.
